title: Six-Component Model of an Information System
description: Interactive K6 graph of the six components of an information system (Hardware, Software, Network, Data, User, Business Process). Students click any component and toggle between three real-world scenarios — hospital admission, loan approval, and university course registration — to see how the same six roles map onto very different organizational tasks.
creator: Information Systems Textbook Project
subject: information systems
systems thinking
six-component model
IS foundations
learningObjective: Given a real-world organizational task, students can identify which entity in the scenario plays each of the six component roles (Hardware, Software, Network, Data, User, Business Process).
audience: Undergraduate students (sophomore/junior) in an introductory Information Systems course.
prerequisites: None beyond the chapter narrative introducing the six components.

educational.audience: Undergraduate sophomores and juniors taking an introductory Information Systems course (CIS, MIS, or general-studies IS).
educational.duration: 5-10 minutes of in-class exploration plus a short writing exercise.
educational.prerequisites: Reading of Chapter 1 sections on the six components of an information system.
educational.learningObjective: Given a real-world organizational task, students can identify which entity in the scenario plays each of the six component roles.
educational.assessment: Students write one paragraph mapping each of the six roles to an organizational task drawn from their own life (e.g., ordering food delivery, returning a library book).

pedagogical.interactionPattern: Click-to-reveal node exploration paired with a scenario toggle. The same six components remain on screen across scenarios; only the right-panel definitions, examples, and what-breaks-if-missing answers change.
pedagogical.designRationale: Apply-level objectives demand transfer of an abstract model to concrete situations. Cycling the same six nodes through three scenarios forces students to recognize structural sameness underneath surface variation — the core Apply move.
pedagogical.systemsThinkingNotes: The complete graph (K6) is itself a teaching device: every component is connected to every other component because in a working IS, every component depends on every other one. The 'what breaks if missing' framing surfaces second-order effects and discourages the rookie mistake of treating any one component in isolation.
